coredata.dc:title: Enhanced singlet oxygen generation of zinc and indium phthalocyanines when conjugated to cysteine functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Atmaca, Göknur Yaşa
Sabbagh, Nazeer Kheer
Celep, Kevser
Şen, Pınar
Şahin, Fikrettin
Erdoğmuş, Ali
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this study is to introduce two new innovative metal phthalocyanines (Pcs); zinc phthalocyanine and indium phthalocyanine, with bromo substituent to use in non invasive approaches for cancer therapy. These approaches include photochemical and sono-photochemical studies in which the generation of singlet oxygen was tested. Halogen substituents are commonly incorporated into phthalocyanine photosensitizers due to the heavy atom effect, where the presence of heavier halogens enhances intersystem crossing (ISC) which leads to a higher yield of triplet-state formation, thereby improving the photosensitizer's efficiency in generating reactive oxygen species. For this purpose, the synthesis of 2‑bromo-4-methylphenol tetra-substituted zinc and indium phthalocyanines was carried out. The obtained new phthalocyanine derivatives were conjugated to cysteine functionalized graphene quantum dots (cys-GQDs) to form supramolecular hybrids through π-π stacking. The photophysical-chemical and sono-photochemical properties of zinc and indium phthalocyanines alone and Pc-conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial were investigated in solutions. All samples demonstrated singlet oxygen generation, with the conjugated complexes showing enhanced production, as observed through the photochemical method. However, the singlet quantum yields produced by the conjugated complexes decreased with sono-photochemical methods compared to the Pcs alone which might be attributed to the possible formation of the non-sono-luminescence sensitive fragments. This study contributes to literature in understanding singlet oxygen generation behavior of conjugated phthalocyanines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc phthalocyanine
Indium phthalocyanine
Sono-photochemical
Photosensitizer
Singlet Oxygen
Graphene Quantum Dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Singlet oxygen efficiency and quantum yields (Φ∆) with photochemical, and sono-photochemical methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized electronic absorption spectra of (a) cys-GQDs, 1, 1@cys-GQDs, (b) cys-GQDs, 2, 2@cys-GQDs.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) showing average particle sizes of (a) cys-GQDs, (b) 1@cys-GQDs, (b) 2@cys-GQDs.
The comparative FT-IR spectra of cys-GQDs, ZnPc (1a), 1@cys-GQDs, InPc (2a) and 2@cys-GQDs (Transmittance (%) versus Wavenumber (cm-1)).
X-ray diffraction patterns of cys-GQDs, ZnPc (1), 1@cys-GQDs, InPc (2) and 2@cys-GQDs.
SEM images of (a) cys-GQDs, (b) ZnPc (1), (c) InPc (2), (d) 1@cys-GQDs, (e) 2@cys-GQDs.
Absorption (695 nm), emission (709 nm) and excitation (697 nm) spectra of complex 1a in DMSO.
Absorption (711 nm), emission (730 nm) and excitation (720 nm) spectra of complex 2a in DMSO.
Change in UV–Vis spectrum of singlet oxygen measurement of complex 1a in a) photochemical study and b) sono-photochemical study.
Change in UV–Vis spectrum of singlet oxygen measurement of complex 2a in a) photochemical study and b) sono-photochemical study.
Change in UV–Vis spectrum of singlet oxygen measurement of complex 1@cys-GQDs in a) photochemical study and b) sono-photochemical study.
Change in UV–Vis spectrum of singlet oxygen measurement of complex 2@cys-GQDs in a) photochemical study and b) sono-photochemical study.
The photodegradation spectrum of complex 1a in DMSO.
The photodegradation spectrum of complex 2 in DMSO.

Synthetic route of a new phthalonitriles (a), (i) DMF, K2CO3, 24 h; and its zinc phthalocyanines derivative (1a), (ii) Pentanol, DBU, zinc (II) acetate, 24 h; and its indium (III) phthalocyanines derivatives (2a), (iii) Pentanol, DBU, indium chloride, 8 h.
Schematic illustration of the π-π stacking of ZnPc (1a) onto graphene quantum dots: (i) sonicate, 4 h, stirred at room temperature, 4 d.
Physicochemical and Sono-photochemical Properties of Different Complexes in DMSO.

head.ce:title: Enhanced singlet oxygen generation of zinc and indium phthalocyanines when conjugated to cysteine functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Health Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence, 34220 Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Health Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eHealth Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence, 34220 Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this study is to introduce two new innovative metal phthalocyanines (Pcs); zinc phthalocyanine and indium phthalocyanine, with bromo substituent to use in non invasive approaches for cancer therapy. These approaches include photochemical and sono-photochemical studies in which the generation of singlet oxygen was tested. Halogen substituents are commonly incorporated into phthalocyanine photosensitizers due to the heavy atom effect, where the presence of heavier halogens enhances intersystem crossing (ISC) which leads to a higher yield of triplet-state formation, thereby improving the photosensitizer's efficiency in generating reactive oxygen species. For this purpose, the synthesis of 2‑bromo-4-methylphenol tetra-substituted zinc and indium phthalocyanines was carried out. The obtained new phthalocyanine derivatives were conjugated to cysteine functionalized graphene quantum dots (cys-GQDs) to form supramolecular hybrids through π-π stacking. The photophysical-chemical and sono-photochemical properties of zinc and indium phthalocyanines alone and Pc-conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial were investigated in solutions. All samples demonstrated singlet oxygen generation, with the conjugated complexes showing enhanced production, as observed through the photochemical method. However, the singlet quantum yields produced by the conjugated complexes decreased with sono-photochemical methods compared to the Pcs alone which might be attributed to the possible formation of the non-sono-luminescence sensitive fragments. This study contributes to literature in understanding singlet oxygen generation behavior of conjugated phthalocyanines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer comes after ischemic heart disease as the second cause of death and expected to be the first in the next half of this century [1,2]. The methods used for cancer treatment were very limited for a long period of time [3,4]. Therefore, recent technologies, such as photodynamic therapy (PDT), sonodynamic therapy (SDT), and sono-photodynamic therapy (SPDT), have been introduced to overcome these challenges [5–8]. These novel approaches offer promising alternatives to traditional methods, aiming for more targeted and effective management of cancer [9,10].

PDT and SDT involves the administration of a sensitizing agent, which selectively accumulates in cancer cells, followed by exposure to a power source of a specific wavelength [7,8,11,12–16]. Activation of the photosensitizer generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), and causes the cell death [11–12,14,15]. PDT is effective for treating superficial skin cancers, including basal cell carcinoma and actinic keratoses [8,11,13]. SDT has the potential for deeper tissue penetration, making it particularly suitable for treating tumors located in challenging anatomical sites or those with substantial depth [17,18]. Additionally, SDT can induce tumor regression, stop tumor progression, and improve patient outcomes [16,19,20]. These approaches provide the ability to selectively target cancer cells, and minimizes the side effects commonly associated with traditional therapies [16,17].

Sono-photodynamic therapy (SPDT) stands at the forefront of innovative cancer treatment strategies, offering a synergistic approach that combines the benefits of PDT and SDT [21,22]. SPDT involves the administration of a photosensitizing agent followed by exposure to ultrasound waves, which activate the photosensitizer to produce ROS [22,23]. SPDT maximizes tumor cell destruction while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues, thereby offering a targeted and precise therapeutic approach [24,25].

The effectiveness of therapy heavily depends on the sensitizer's characteristics, such as its absorption spectrum, photostability, and tissue penetration depth [26,27]. Thus, the selection of an appropriate photosensitizer is critical for its success [27]. Phthalocyanines are commonly used as photosensitizers in clinical settings. They exhibit potent antitumor activity against various cancer cell lines through their ability to generate reactive oxygen species under light activation, making them effective photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy [28,29]. When combined with metal ions, they efficiently incorporate into target cells while remaining non-toxic to healthy cells [30]. Metals such as zinc and indium, phthalocyanines have shown significant promise in PDT, SPDT applications which allows for deeper tissue penetration [24].

In addition to these strategies, nanotechnology offers very effective method in drug delivery. Additionally, nanotechnology improves solubility, biodistribution, and cellular uptake. Combining nanotechnology with PDT provides promising alternative to traditional cancer treatments [31–33]. One of the material that is used recently in nantech is Graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs are the next generation of carbon based nanomaterials. They are widely used as theranostic platforms due to their outstanding physical, chemical and biological properties. Their non-toxic and biologically inert properties at cellular level make them the future of nanomedicine and biotechnology [34]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) play a crucial role in cancer treatment by inducing oxidative stress, which activates both apoptosis (type I) and autophagy (type II), making them promising agents for photodynamic therapy (PDT) [35]. The two-dimensional architecture and chemical composition of GQDs offer several options for the attachment of Pcs as drugs. The Pcs can be linked by covalent bonds on the edge of the GQDs or by noncovalent interactions on the surface of the GQDs [36]. However, the attaching of the Pcs via the covalent linkers can change the photophysical properties of Pcs, thus decreasing its phototoxicity. Therefore, another strategy including the GQDs as carrier and Pcs as drugs have focused on noncovalent interactions through the π–π stacking occuring between the π -systems of Pcs and monolayer sheets of graphene rich in π electrons, resulting in superior photodynamic properties of such formulations [37].

In this study, taking into consideration the tunable properties of GQDs, we carried out the functionalization at edge side of the GQDs by cysteine. Then, the obtained new zinc and indium phthalocyanines carrying the 2‑bromo-4-methylphenol substituents were conjugated to the cystein-functionalized GQDs (cys-GQDs) to modulate the photophysical and photosensitizing properties of the drugs with the expectation of enhanced singlet oxygen generation. The photosensing potential of the new zinc and indium phthalocyanines and their nanodrug formulations were evaluated by their photochemical, and sono-photochemical properties. This research seeks to determine the suitability of these compounds as efficient sono-photosensitizers for various application methods.

The experimental procedure to synthesise compound a was explained in detail in the supplementary material. IR spectrum (cm-1): 3100–3150 (Ar., CH), 2980–2860 (Alip., CH), 2230 (CN), 1620, 1590 (C = C), 1484,1459 (CC), 1280 (CO-C), 1046 (CO-C), 790 (C-Br). MS (MALDI-MS) m/z: Calculated: 313.154 for C15H9BrN2O; Found:313.896 [M]+, 1H NMR (500 MHz, δppm, CDCI3): 7.56 (m, 1H, Ar-H), 7.53 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.48 (d, 1H, Ar-H), 7.24 (d, 1H, Ar-H), 7.10 (d, 1H, Ar-H), 6.89 (d, 1H, Ar-H), 2.41 (s, 3H, -CH3).

The experimental procedure to synthesise compound 1a was explained in detail in the supplementary material. FT-IR νmax/cm−1(ATR): 3031 (Ar-CH), 2954–2851 (Aliph-CH), 1592 (C = C), 1471–1374 (Aliph-C-C), 1304 (CN), 1244 (asym., CO-C), 1045 (sym., CO-C), 770 (C-Br). MS (MALDI-MS) m/z: Calculated: 1318.00 for C60H36Br4N8O4Zn; Found:1318.06 [M]+. 1H NMR (500 MHz, δppm, CDCI3): 7.60 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 7.56 (s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.52 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 7.19 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 7.11 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 6.87 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 2.40 (s, 12H, -CH3). Uv-Vis (DMSO): Q Band at 695 nm and B Band at 327 nm.

The experimental procedure to synthesise compound 2a was explained in detail in the supplementary material. FT-IR νmax/cm−1(ATR): 3031 (Ar-CH), 2959–2853 (Aliph-CH), 1585 (C = C), 1473–1378 (Aliph-C-C), 1301 (CN), 1246 (asym., CO-C), 1045 (sym., CO-C), 753 (C-Br). MS (MALDI-MS) m/z: Calculated: 1402.886 for C60H36Br4InN8O4CI: 1369.06 [M-CI+2H]+. 1H NMR (500 MHz, δppm, CDCI3): 7.60 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 7.57 (s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.51 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 7.18 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 7.11 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 6.87 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 2.39 (s, 12H, -CH3). Uv-Vis (DMSO): Q Band at 711 nm and B Band at 365 nm.

Cys-GQDs were synthesized using citric acid as a carbon source and Cysteine amino acid as a sulfur and nitrogen source using the literature method with some modification [38]. Briefly, 1 g citric acid and 0.5 g cysteine were dissolved in 3 mL of DI water. The obtained solution was hydrothermally treated at 220 °C for 12 h under N2 gas. After that, the reddish-brown mixture was filtered. This suspension was purified with a dialysis membrane (12–14 kD, Fisher Scientific) against DI water for 24 h and then precipitated in excess amounts of acetone by using a centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, cys-GQDs were dried in an oven at 50 °C and stored in a closed container for further applications.

The Pcs (1a and 2a) conjugation of cys-GQDs based on π-π stacking was performed by way of a slight modification to the previously reported literature [39]. Briefly, the solution of 12.5 mg of cys-GQDs (in 1 mL H2O) and the solutions of 25 mg of 1a (0,5 mL in DMF) and 25 mg 2a (0,5 mL in DMF) were sonicated for 1 h separately. Then, cys-GQDs solution was added to the solutions of 1a and 2a, separately, and the mixture was sonicated for 4 h then stirred at room temperature for 4 d The mixture was precipitated with ethanol and washed with water and ethanol. The product was dried in a laboratory oven at 60 °C to obtain Pc@cys-GQDs. The obtained nanocomposites are represented as 1@ cys-GQDs and 2@ cys-GQDs.
The methods and the experimental set-up to perform the photophysical, photochemical and sono-photochemical studies are presented in the supplementary material.

Scheme 1 illustrates the process of zinc and indium phthalocyanine derivatives synthesis, these derivatives were characterized by the UV–Vis, FT-IR, 1HNMR and mass spectroscopy techniques (Fig. S1–7). Scheme 2 represents the preparation of the conjugates. For the characterization of the conjugates XRD, FT-IR, dynamic light scattering (DLS) and UV/vis analytical techniques were employed.

The characterization of the synthesized 2‑bromo-4-methylphenol tetra substituted zinc and indium phthalocyanines were performed using various analytical techniques to confirm its structure and composition.

The 1H NMR spectrum of 1a was recorded to identify the proton environments within the molecule. Multiple split signals were observed at chemical shifts of 7.60, 7.56, 7.52, 7.19, 7.11 and 6.87 ppm. These signals correspond to the aromatic protons on the zinc phthalocyanine ring and the aromatic protons of the ligand. Additionally, the aliphatic protons of the ligands were observed at 2.40 ppm, confirming the presence of the expected substituent groups on the phthalocyanine structure.

The mass spectrum of 1a provided a peak at 1318.06, which is in excellent agreement with the calculated molecular mass of 1318.00 for the compound with the formula C60H36Br4N8O4Zn. This result confirms the successful synthesis of the targeted molecule, as the observed molecular ion peak corresponds with the expected molecular structure.

In the FT-IR spectrum of compound 1a, the characteristic CN peak disappeared due to the formation of phthalocyanine. The CO-C vibration was observed at 1244 cm-1. The aromatic CH peak was seen at 3031 cm-1. Other vibrations were observed at 2954–2851 cm-1 for Aliphatic-CH, 1592 cm-1 for C = C, 1340 cm-1 for CN and 770 cm-1 for C-Br. In Uv-Vis spectroscopy results, compound 1a gave the specific phthalocyanine Q and B bands at 695 nm and 327 nm respectively due to π → π * transitions [40]. These spectral features confirm the structural integrity and the presence of the expected functional groups in the synthesized complex.

The 1H NMR spectrum of compound 2a was recorded to identify the proton environments within the molecule. Multiple split signals were observed at chemical shifts ranging from 6.87 to 7.60 ppm. These signals correspond to the aromatic protons on the indium phthalocyanine ring and the aromatic protons of the ligand. Additionally, the aliphatic protons of the ligands were observed at 2.39 ppm, confirming the presence of the expected substituent groups on the phthalocyanine structure.

The mass spectrum of 2a provided a peak at 1369.06 [M-CI+2H]+, which is in excellent agreement with the calculated molecular mass of 1367.41 for the compound with the formula C60H36Br4N8O4InCI. This result confirms the successful synthesis of the targeted molecule, as the observed molecular ion peak corresponds with the expected molecular structure.

In the FT-IR spectrum of 2, the characteristic CN peak disappeared due to the formation of phthalocyanine. The CO-C vibration was observed at 1246 cm-1. The aromatic CH peak was seen at 3031 cm-1. Other vibrations were observed at 2959–2853 cm-1 for Aliphatic-CH, 1585 cm-1 for C = C, 1473–1378 cm-1 for Aliph-C-C, 1301 cm-1C-N, and 753 cm-1 for C-Br. In Uv-Vis spectroscopy results, compound 2a gave the specific phthalocyanine Q and B bands at 711 nm and 365 nm respectively due to π → π * transitions [40]. These spectral features confirm the structural integrity and the presence of the expected functional groups in the synthesized complex 2a.

In the UV spectrum of the prepared nanoconjugates (1@cys-GQDs and 2@ cys-GQDs) were shown in Fig. 1 . together with the Pcs (1a and 2a) and cys-GQDs alone for comparison purposes. The UV–vis spectrum of cys-GQDs exhibits absorption peaks at 344 and 392 nm which can be attributed to aromatic π–π* transition and n–π* transition of aromatic moieties and heteroatom functional groups, respectively [41]. Upon conjugation occured, the nanoconjugates displayed absorption bands confirming the presence of Pcs (1a and 2a) and the cys-GQDs (Fig. 1a and 1b). There were no shifts in the Q-bands of the 1@cys-GQDs and only 2 nm shifting in the Q-bands of the 2@cys-GQDs upon π-π coordination of Pcs (1a and 2a) to the respective GQDs compared to Pcs alone. It was confirmed that there was a negligible change in the Q bands upon coordination of Pcs with carbon nanomaterials [42]. However, there was an increase in absorbance at the B band region of the spectra due to the intense absorbance of cys-GQDs (Fig. 1a, 1b).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to observe the distribution and shape of the cys-GQDs before and after conjugation to Pcs (1a,2a). The TEM displayed the image of the cys-GQDs (Fig. 2a ) to be spherical graphene quantum dots and monodispersed (Fig. 2a). Upon the non-covalent (π-π stacking) interaction of cys-GQDs and Pcs (1a and 2a), the visible particle-like dispersions for the 1@cys-GQDs (Fig. 2a) and 2@cys-GQDs (Fig. 2b) is the result of the formation of clusters due to interaction of neighboring nanoparticles. Aggregation of phthalocyanines are known upon covalent and/or non-covalent interactions with each other and/or nanoparticles [43].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out to determine the size of the cys-GQDs before and after conjugation to Pcs (1a,2a). The average size determined by DLS was 7.8 nm for cys-GQDs (Fig. 3a ). The observed size distribution is in accordance with typical GQDs [44]. The hydrodynamic sizes for the Ps nanoconjugates were found to be higher than that recorded for the cys-GQDs alone at an average size of 27.08 nm for 1@cys-GQDs and 29.54 nm for 2@cys-GQDs. This increase in size indicates the formation of supramolecular assemblies between the cys-GQDs and the Pcs (1a, 2a) and in accordance with the TEM images of the nanoconjugates.

Zeta potentials of cys-GQDs, 1@cys-GQDs and 2@cys-GQDs were measured. cys-GQDs gives a negative Zeta potential value of −24.38 mV indicating negative charges attached on the surface of cys-GQDs [45]. The conjugates 1@cys-GQDs, 2@cys-GQDs forming the nanoassembly between the ZnPc (1) and InPc (2) exhibited the low zeta potential of −10.2 and −8.4 mV, respectively, showing potential for aggregation [46].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was further also utilized to characterize the cys-GQDs and the cys-GQDs–Pc conjugates (Fig. 4 ). The FTIR spectrum of cys-GQDs exhibited the functional groups localized at the edges/surface of the graphene sheet. The band at 1709 cm–1 for cys-GQDs is attributed to C = O vibration of the carboxyl group. The broad band arising in the range 3402−3196 cm−1 is due to the presence of functional groups like -OH and NH2. The peaks at 1635, 1530, 1393, 1232 and 1204 cm−1 can be attributed to NH bending, C = C, CN, CO and C-S stretching vibrations. In addition, two bands at 2986 cm−1 and 2939 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of –CH2 groups.Upon conjugation, C = O vibration of the carboxyl group was seen at 1716 cm−1 for 1@cys-GQDs and 1710 cm−1 for 2@cys-GQDs. OH vibrations revealed at 3203 cm−1 for 1@cys-GQDs and 3430 cm−1 for 2@cys-GQDs. The peaks arising from the C = C, CN, CO and C-S stretching vibrations were observed at 1611, 1387, 1245, 1203 cm−1 and 1608, 1388, 1251, 1200 cm−1 for 1@cys-GQDs and 2@cys-GQDs, respectively. The peaks belonging to the Pcs alone (1a and 2a) were observed at the FT-IR of the conjugates confirming the loading of the Pcs (1a and 2a) onto the cys-GQDs.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared cys-GQDs, ZnPc (1a) and InPc (2a) and their XRD pattern after conjugation are shown in Fig. 5 . The XRD diffractogram of cys-GQDs shows a main broad band with a maximum at approximately 2Ɵ = 21.99° which is due to (002) Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers [47]. The broadness of the diffraction peaks is an indication of the small size of the quantum dots [43].

As observed in the XRD of the cys-GQDs, the broad peak observed for phthalocyanine positioned at 2Ɵ = 22.62° for 1a and 2Ɵ = 23.39° for 1a is close to the 002 reflection plane of carbon which is the major constituent of phthalocyanines showing the amorphous structure [48]. Also, there are several sharp but small diffraction peaks at 2Ɵ = 27.02° and 2Ɵ =31.05° for 1a, 2Ɵ = 25.74° and 2Ɵ = 30.62° for 2a, which indicates the samples may undergo a crystallization process. 1@cys-GQDs showed similar diffraction peaks at 2Ɵ = 21.44° and 2Ɵ = 27.65 as shifted as compared to cys-GQDs and ZnPc (1a) indicating indicate the interaction between them. 2@cys-GQDs exhibited the similar broad peak at between 2Ɵ =20–40° but the sharp peaks embedded in the patterns at 2Ɵ =16.78 o, 27.50 o, 33.89 o, 43.87 o and 47.50 o is a clear indication of new complexes formation between cys-GQDs and 2a [47].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to explore the morphology of the prepared products. Fig. 6a shows the SEM image of cys-GQDs in which we see that the crystals are densely nucleated and self-assembled into small voluminous cylindrical structure [49]. In the SEM image of the ZnPc (1) (Fig. 6b) and InPc (2) (Fig. 6c) revealed the fibrous structure with small porous looking. The SEM images of the 1@cys-GQDs (Fig. 6d) and 2@cys-GQDs (Fig. 6e) exhibited the an undefined geometric shapes inferring that ZnPc (1) and InPc (2) are well embedded in the cys-GQDs [50].

In this section photophysical and photochemical results of the molecules are presented comparing the synthesized zinc and indium phthalocyanines with their quantum dot derivatives and treatment methods ‘photochemical- sono-photochemical’ including photodegradation and fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Singlet oxygen efficiency and quantum yields (Φ∆) with photochemical, and sono-photochemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: Singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ) is a key parameter for evaluating singlet oxygen production efficiency, particularly in photodynamic therapy (PDT). In this study, ΦΔ values were determined in DMSO using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a singlet oxygen quencher. The degradation rate of DPBF, monitored via UV–Vis spectroscopy, is directly proportional to singlet oxygen generation, providing a quantitative measure of the photosensitizer's efficiency. To assess singlet oxygen production, sono-photosensitizers were exposed to light alone and in combination with ultrasound. For photochemical studies, phthalocyanine complexes were irradiated at 5-second intervals with a light intensity of 7.05 × 10¹⁵ photons s⁻¹ cm⁻². The resulting singlet oxygen quantum yields (Φ∆PDT) were 0.46 for complex 1a, 0.54 for complex 2a, 0.62 for 1@cys-GQDs and 0.78 for 2@cys-GQDs (Table 1). According to these results, the singlet oxygen quantum yield values for complex 1a, 1@cys-GQDs and 2a are lower than unsubstituted ZnPc (Φ∆PDT = 0.67) while 2@cys-GQDs’s value is higher by 16 %. Complex 2a and its quantum dot derivative overall yielded better results than complex 1a as expected due to the presence of indium in their structure, as a heavier atom than zinc, increasing the intersystem crossing more and therefore increasing singlet oxygen generation.

Both compounds’ quantum dot derivatives yielded improved results (35 % higher for complex 1a, 45 % higher for complex 2a) confirming the quantum dot’s contribution to singlet oxygen production. Fluorescence quenching of aromatic molecules by graphene sheets has been reported due to the fact that the S1 excited state of Pc moiety in Pc–graphene complex is quenched somewhat by the bound graphene, which is the intramolecular interaction [58]. This case could cause more efficient intersystem crossing to the triplet state, resulting in the more populated triplet excited photo sensitizer in this state and accordingly, more singlet oxygen quantum yield [59].

Although it is known that the sono method involves a mechanism similar to PDT, in the case of chemical pyrolysis (sono-decomposition), violently imploding microbubbles can cause the nearby sono-sensitizer to break, releasing high energy and pressure in the environment, which can lead to the formation of sono-sensitizer-derived radicals, leading to the formation of non-sono-luminescence sensitive fragments. In this case, low singlet oxygen yield can be encountered [60]. Overall, the highest singlet oxygen generation in this study is observed at sono-photochemical studies of complex 2a while the lowest singlet oxygen generation is observed at photochemical studies of complex 1a.

Additionally, Table 1 lists previously published data on several zinc and indium phthalocyanines. The quantum yields obtained in this research are compared with those of other synthesized compounds reported in the literature. The achieved Φ∆PDT results of compounds 2, 1@cys-GQDs and 2@cys-GQDs are higher than the existing data which differ in the range of 0.27 to 0.52 [51,53,54,56,57] while Φ∆PDT results of compounds 1a, 2a and 1@cys-GQDs are lower than [52] and [55] halogen substituted zinc phthalocyanines. When the Φ∆SPDT results of the compounds are compared to the rest of the given data, all values achieved in this study were higher than [56] and lower than [55]. Considering these results, the newly synthesized phthalocyanines in this study exhibit significantly high quantum yields, therefore, they demonstrate potential for use as sensitizers in cancer treatment through photodynamic and sono-photodynamic therapy.

The use of graphene quantum dots in the synthesis of anticancer agents is gaining attention due to their biological properties. Specifically, because the fluorescence of aromatic molecules is quenched by the graphene layers they interact with, the rate of intersystem transitions to triplet state increases, and, as a result, an increase in singlet oxygen yield is expected. For this purpose, this study aimed to synthesize the new ZnPc (1a) and InPc (2a) bearing tetra-2-Bromo-4-methylphenol and their nanoconjugates (1@cys-GQDs, 2@cys-GQDs) as sensitizer candidates. Besides, the photophysical-chemical and sono-photochemical properties of complexes 1a, 2a and their GQDs derivatives were determined including ΦF, ΦΔ with photochemical, and sono-photochemical methods and Φd, comparatively.

The photodynamic therapy (PDT) and sono-photodynamic therapy (SPDT) results indicate that the molecular structure significantly influences the singlet oxygen yield. Comparing 1a and 2a, the indium-centered phthalocyanine (2a) exhibited a 17.4 % higher PDT yield (0.54 vs. 0.46) and a 4.3 % higher SPDT yield (0.96 vs. 0.92). This increase can be attributed to the heavy atom effect of indium, which enhances intersystem crossing efficiency, thereby increasing singlet oxygen generation in both photochemical and sono-photochemical methods. When evaluating the effect of the applied therapeutic method, it was observed that all molecules demonstrated improved singlet oxygen generation under SPDT compared to PDT. Specifically, SPDT resulted in a 100 % increase in 1a (0.92 vs. 0.46) and a 77.8 % increase in 2a (0.96 vs. 0.54). This suggests that ultrasound activation plays a crucial role in enhancing the therapeutic potential of phthalocyanines by increasing ROS production.

Furthermore, when comparing the effect of graphene quantum dot (GQD) conjugation, both 1@cys-GQDs and 2@cys-GQDs exhibited contrasting behaviors under PDT and SPDT conditions. Under PDT, the GQD-conjugated phthalocyanines showed a significant increase in singlet oxygen generation, with 1@cys-GQDs increasing by 34.8 % (0.62 vs. 0.46) and 2@cys-GQDs increasing by 44.4 % (0.78 vs. 0.54), indicating that GQD enhances light absorption and electron transfer. However, under SPDT, a reverse trend was observed, where singlet oxygen generation decreased upon conjugation, with 1@cys-GQDs showing a 19.6 % reduction (0.74 vs. 0.92) and 2@cys-GQDs showing a 12.5 % reduction (0.84 vs. 0.96). This decrease is likely due to the formation of non-sono-luminescence-sensitive fragments upon conjugation, which may interfere with ultrasound-induced energy transfer.

These findings highlight the importance of molecular design and therapeutic method selection in optimizing phthalocyanine-based PDT and SPDT applications. With their high singlet oxygen generation efficiency and adaptability to different activation methods, these sensitizer molecules hold great potential for advancing next-generation cancer therapies with improved selectivity and treatment efficacy.

Göknur Yaşa Atmaca: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Nazeer Kheer Sabbagh: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Kevser Celep: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Pınar Şen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fikrettin Şahin: Writing – review & editing. Ali Erdoğmuş: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Kırbaç, A. Erdoğmuş, New non-peripherally substituted zinc phthalocyanines; synthesis, and comparative photophysicochemical properties. J. Mol. Struct., 1202 (2020)127392. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molstruc.2019.127392.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Acar, Z.Bıyıklıoğlu, M. Durmuş, H. Kantekin, Synthesis, characterization and comparative studies on the photophysical and photochemical properties of peripherally and non-peripherally tetra-substituted zinc (II) phthalocyanines. J. Organomet. Chem., 708 (2012) 65–74. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jorganchem.2012.02.028.

Papers, 77(10) (2023) 5721–5731. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11696-023-02892-3

Struct., 1250 (2022) 131850. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.131850.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Sen, T. ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.Y. Atmaca, C.C. Karanlık, A. Erdoğmuş, Measurement of improved singlet oxygen generations of indium chloride phthalocyanines by comparatively sono-photochemical and photochemical studies, Dyes Pigments. 194 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of substituents on the photochemical and photophysical properties of main group metal phthalocyanines
